facts_disclosed: Great relationship with hospital OBs, esp Dr Singh

turns.content: i want to write a thank you email to a colleague and i'm struggling with the tone. i don't want it to sound sappy but i genuinely want to acknowledge what she does

That's a nice thing to do, and the tone concern is valid — you want it to feel genuine without being over the top. Can you tell me a bit about the relationship? Is this a peer, a supervisor, someone you collaborate with? And what specifically do you want to thank her for?

she's an obstetrician at the hospital where i bring my mamas for births. i'm a community midwife so we work together when things need medical input but we're not in the same team exactly. the dynamic between midwives and OBs can be really fraught in a lot of places but the obstetricians at christchurch women's are genuinely good to work alongside eh. and this one in particular, Dr Singh, she's something else

That midwife-OB dynamic is famously difficult in a lot of hospitals, so it says something that you feel strongly enough to write a thank you. What is it about Dr Singh specifically that you want to acknowledge? Is it a recent incident, or more of an ongoing thing?

it's ongoing really but she had a moment last week that kind of crystallised it for me. one of my mamas needed an emergency consult and Dr Singh came in and the first thing she did was sit down and ask my mama what she wanted. not what the protocol said, not what the chart showed. she asked the mama. and then she turned to me and asked what my clinical assessment was before she gave hers. that respect for midwifery-led care is so rare from an obstetrician eh

That's a powerful example — sitting down, centring the mama, and respecting your clinical judgment before asserting her own. Those small actions completely change the power dynamic in the room. And you're right, that kind of respect from an OB is rare enough that it's worth naming.

For the email, I think the key is to be specific about what she did and why it mattered, without generalising too much. Something like:

- Name the specific moment (the consult last week)
- Say what she did that stood out (asked the mama first, deferred to your assessment)
- Say why it matters (to you as a midwife, to the mama, to the model of care)

Would you like me to draft something you can edit into your own voice?

yeah that would be great. and i want to be careful because i don't want it to sound like i'm surprised she's good at her job. it's more that i appreciate her approach because i've seen the alternative at other hospitals where the OB walks in and takes over and the midwife basically disappears. Dr Singh never does that. she treats it like a genuine collaboration

Right — appreciation, not surprise. That's an important distinction. Here's a draft you can reshape:

---

Hi Dr Singh,

I wanted to take a moment to acknowledge something from last week's consult with [mama's name]. The way you approached that situation — sitting with her, asking what she wanted, and checking in with me before giving your assessment — made a real difference in that room.

I know that might sound like I'm just describing good practice, and it is. But I've worked in enough settings to know that it's not the norm. The collaborative approach you bring to working with midwives genuinely changes the experience for our mamas, and it makes me a better practitioner.

I feel fortunate to work alongside someone who values midwifery-led care the way you do. Thank you.

Aroha

---

It's direct, specific, and doesn't overdo it. How does that feel as a starting point?

that's really close actually. i'd soften the language a bit because it reads a little formal for how we actually talk to each other. we're pretty relaxed, she calls me by my first name and i call her Priya. but the bones of it are perfect. the line about it not being the norm is important because it's true and she probably doesn't hear it enough

Good — if you're on first-name terms and the relationship is relaxed, the email should reflect that. Here's a softer version:

---

Hi Priya,

Just wanted to say something about last week's consult with [mama]. The way you handled that — sitting down with her, asking what she wanted first, then checking in with me — it really mattered. Not just for the clinical outcome but for how she felt in that room.

I've worked with OBs who walk in and take the reins without a backward glance, so I don't take for granted that you treat it as a genuine partnership. It makes a huge difference to my mamas and to me.

Appreciate you.

Aroha

---

Shorter, warmer, more conversational. You can tweak from there.

oh that's much better. the appreciate you at the end is nice, that's how i'd actually close it. i might add something about looking forward to the next complex case together because honestly when things get tricky she's the first person i want in the room. not because i can't handle it but because having someone who trusts your judgment makes everything calmer for the mama

That's a great addition — it shifts the email from just looking back to looking forward, which feels more like an ongoing relationship than a one-off thank you. Something like:

"Next time something tricky comes through, you're the first person I want in the room. Not because I need rescuing — because you make the whole thing calmer."

That captures what you're saying without undermining your own capability. Want me to slot that in, or are you happy to edit from here?

no i'll take it from here. honestly i could probably just send what you've got with a couple of tweaks. it says what i want to say without being over the top. i feel like we don't say these things enough to the people who make our work better eh. midwifery can be really isolating and having that one person at the hospital who actually gets it makes such a difference

You're absolutely right — people rarely hear the good stuff. Everyone's quick to complain or escalate, but a genuine thank you for how someone shows up in their work? That lands differently. And for someone like Dr Singh who's clearly intentional about how she collaborates, knowing that a midwife notices and values it probably means a lot.

Send it. She'll be glad you did.
sending it now before i overthink it. thanks for the help, this was much easier than staring at a blank email for 20 minutes which is where i was before. kia kaha

Ha, the blank email spiral is real. Glad you've got something that feels right. And the fact that you took the time to do this says a lot about you as a colleague too. Kia kaha to you as well — take care.
